rationale: The citing paper discusses Draganska and Klapper (2011) in multiple core methodological passages, not just as background. It uses that study as a direct comparator for modeling advertising effects on awareness/choice sets, explicitly stating that Draganska and Klapper treat the effect as essentially static and rely on micro choice-set survey data. The citing paper then positions its own contribution as extending this line by modeling dynamic awareness evolution and identifying advertising channels with aggregate data and a dynamic supply side.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Draganska and Klapper (2011)'s awareness/choice-set approach and decomposition of advertising effects, but extends it to a dynamic awareness process over product life cycles. It argues that the prior paper's static treatment is insufficient for new durable products and develops a dynamic structural alternative.
